title: Sum of Uniform Random Variables

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: X_1, X_2, ..., X_n are i.i.d. Uniform[0, 1] random variables. Let S_n = X_1 + X_2 + ... + X_n.
rules: Each X_i is uniform on [0, 1], independent of the others.
setup.question: What is P(S_n < 1)?

learningObjectives: Recognize P(S_n < 1) as the volume of the n-dimensional simplex {x : x_i >= 0, sum x_i < 1}.
Compute small cases (n=1, 2, 3) by direct geometry.

stages.label: Compute small cases
stages.explanation: n=1: P(X_1 < 1) = 1. n=2: triangle with vertices (0,0),(1,0),(0,1), area 1/2. n=3: tetrahedron with vertices (0,0,0),(1,0,0),(0,1,0),(0,0,1), volume 1/6.

stages.label: Conjecture and prove by induction
stages.prompt: Conjecture: P(S_n < 1) = 1/n!. Inductive step: assume P(S_n < 1) = 1/n!. Compute P(S_{n+1} < 1) by conditioning on X_{n+1} = x.

options.text: P(S_{n+1} < 1) = integral from 0 to 1 of P(S_n < 1 - x) dx = integral from 0 to 1 of (1 - x)^n / n! dx = 1/(n+1)!.

stages.explanation: P(S_n < 1 - x) = (1 - x)^n / n! by scaling (the simplex with side a has volume a^n / n!). Integrate from 0 to 1: result is [-(1 - x)^(n+1) / ((n+1) * n!)] from 0 to 1 = 1/(n+1)!. Induction complete.

stages.label: Geometric interpretation

options.text: The VOLUME of the n-dimensional unit simplex {x in R^n : x_i >= 0, sum x_i < 1}, which equals 1/n!.
The volume of the unit cube.
The surface area of a sphere.

stages.explanation: The unit simplex in n dimensions has vertices at the origin and the n unit basis vectors. Its volume is 1/n! -- a famous formula.

stages.label: Commit for n = 5
stages.explanation: P(S_5 < 1) = 1/5! = 1/120 ≈ 0.00833. Probability decays factorially -- once you sum a few uniforms, the chance the sum stays under 1 vanishes quickly.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What's E[S_n]? Compare to 1 to predict whether P(S_n < 1) is large or small.
Use the SAME technique to find the volume of the unit simplex in R^n.
What's the probability sum of n exponentials is less than 1? (Different distribution but similar ideas.)
